metadata.title: Using Structural Topic Modeling to Explore the Role of Framing in Shaping the Debate on Liquefied Natural Gas Terminals in Oregon

abstract: The drastic increase in domestic production of natural gas due to the fracking boom prompted efforts to develop a robust infrastructure in the U.S. to export natural gas. Given environmental concerns over increased fossil fuel development, significant opposition mobilized to "keep it [fossil fuels] in the ground" by acting to prevent not only natural gas production but also its transportation via pipelines and shipping via export terminals. Our analysis focuses on the latter component, specifically examining the long history of proposed liquefied natural gas infrastructure in two coastal communities in Oregon. Members of the public engaged in the formal siting processes and mobilized both opposition and support. We examine their use of collective action frames in both comments at public hearings and letters to the editor in local newspapers (N = 4618) over the 16 years that these proposals were under consideration, quantifying the dynamic nature of framing using computational text analysis. We find that both groups vary their use of framing over time and by venue, reacting to exogenous events (e.g., September 11th, Fukushima) and tailor their messages to the context (e.g., an in-person hearing or a letter to the editor). Opponents concentrated on potential threats, initially emphasizing local impacts like tanker and pipeline safety but eventually focusing on climate concerns. paragraphs: The growth in U.S. natural gas production due to the combination of hydraulic fracturing (or fracking) and horizontal directional drilling has led to a related expansion in infrastructure proposals beyond the extraction site, including both pipelines and export terminals designed to transport natural gas to international markets. Concerns about the extraction process itself, associated infrastructure, and climate impacts of a continued reliance on fossil fuels have combined to create local and regional resistance to these proposals-perhaps the most well-known to the Keystone XL pipeline (e.g., Gravelle & Lachapelle, 2015). A growing group of interconnected activists around the U.S. and world are working to "keep it (fossil fuel) in the ground" or "leave it in the ground"-in part by targeting specific proposals for fossil fuel development, including this associated infrastructure. Environmental and climate activists are thus pushing countries to move beyond national climate policies that focus on the site of combustion of fossil fuels (i.e., demand) and explicitly target the supply side of fossil fuel development-for example, sites of extraction and infrastructure like terminals that facilitate the global trade of fossil fuels (Piggot, 2018;Erickson et al., 2018). Mobilization against these proposals has become an important part of the wider environmental movement as concerns about the climate crisis have grown in recent years.

Both scholars of energy facility siting and social movement studies have identified frames and the framing process as important in mobilizing interested and bystander publics in efforts to influence decision makers (e.g., Clarke et al., 2015;Walsh et al., 1997), as well as shaping the public's understanding of the benefits and risks of controversial technologies and proposals (e.g., Matthews & Hansen, 2018). In essence, frames and the framing work done by activists, elites, adversaries, the media, etc., around an issue focus attention on particular aspects of that issue, weave these aspects together into a story, and potentially transform how one sees the issue.

While the debate around fracking has received quite a bit of attention in the media and from scholars, less is known about the framing of associated infrastructure, particularly export terminals. Such facilities can create both benefits and risks to the communities that host them-with benefits often coming in economic terms (e.g., local tax revenues, community enhancement or investment plans, and jobs) and risks in social or environmental terms (e.g., dredging for ship clearance, public safety in the event of an accident, and industrialization of the coast). Yet, as we know from previous studies, frames develop in and are shaped by context and place. Here, we explore the framing on both sides of the debate over six proposals for liquefied natural gas (LNG) import and export terminals in Oregon using computational text analysis to examine more than 4500 comments in letters to the editor and at public hearings from 2004 to 2019. The Oregon proposals were among the most contentious in the U.S. and present a unique opportunity to better understand how opponents and supporters frame such proposals over time and by venue, and how these frames relate to local, regional, and national issues.

Our analysis seeks to build upon previous case study research of energy facility siting by employing new computational text analysis methods to explore issue framing by both opponents and supporters and how it varies over time and context. Framing is a powerful tool for social movement actors, but research around framing has predominately relied upon qualitative content analysis. Our use of machine learning techniques such as topic modeling allows us to perform similar analysis at scale, processing large quantities of text over time.

paragraphs: We begin by reviewing relevant literatures related to framing more generally and the discourse around fracking and other fossil fuel development more specifically.

Framing. Issue frames highlight a particular part of the world and provide the organizing idea that holds together a collection of symbols, images, and arguments (Ryan & Gamson, 2016). The concept of framing has been employed by a wide range of scholars in different fields, including social movement studies (Ryan & Gamson, 2016;Snow & Benford, 1988), organizational studies (e.g., Kaplan, 2008), and political communication (Chong & Druckman, 2007).

In the field of social movement studies, Snow and Benford's (1988) seminal piece outlined the basic principles of framing in the context of collective action. They identified three core tasks of collective action frames: (1) diagnose an event as in need of changing (diagnostic); (2) propose solutions to the diagnosed problem (prognostic); and (3) call to action (motivational). Snow and Benford hypothesized that successful mobilization is contingent upon attending to all three framing tasks. Collective action frames also vary across dimensions such as problem identification, flexibility/ inclusivity, scope, and resonance (Benford & Snow, 2000). The development of a frame is dynamic and influenced by three overlapping processes. First, frames are developed through speech acts and written communication in the context of movement activities, whereby the frame is articulated by the connection of events and experiences in a way that emphasizes certain aspects as more salient than others. Second, frames are also deployed strategically to achieve a specific purpose, such as through frame bridging that links congruent but unconnected frames. Third, frames are developed through contested processes, as all those involved in a movement or counter-movement exchange frames with all other actors and therefore adapt frames as a result of framing contests.

Framing involves a "strategic dialogue intended to shape a particular group into a coherent movement" (Ryan & Gamson, 2016, p. 13). When grounded in an understanding of power relations, strategic use of framing can build and sustain a movement. Ryan and Gamson elaborated four key points about frames: (1) facts take on meaning by how they are embedded in frames; (2) people carry around multiple frames for an issue or event in their heads; (3) successful reframing involves understanding an adversary's worldview; and (4) all frames contain some appeal to moral principle, either implicit or explicit. However, Ryan and Gamson also caution that framing alone is insufficient to build a movement and efforts must be made within the context of movement-building and focusing on the carriers of frames, such as using framing to determine how to gain access or to facilitate collaboration between groups with similar goals.

More recent research on framing has sought to examine a wide range of social movements-at both the national and international level-and expand our understanding of framing strategies. For example, the concept of frame resonance has been further explored, particularly by examining the mechanisms through which frames fail to resonate, such as misalignment between frame and audience or exhaustion with overused or taken-for-granted frames (Snow & Corrigall-Brown, 2005). Other research has focused on how framing is used in resource mobilization by identifying why certain frames are emphasized to audiences from which an organization is hoping to receive donations (Wooten, 2010). Scholarship in this vein has focused on framing as a dynamic process that is always evolving-a departure from previous studies that too often treated frames as static (Benford, 1997).

In fact, Benford (1997) has critiqued past approaches to studying the role of framing in social movements, specifically noting that, "we lack systematic empirical studies across cases, movements, and time" (p. 411). In this article, we use computational text analysis to do just this-study the framing of LNG in Oregon over time and across cases in a systematic manner.

Discourse around fracking and fossil fuel development. Discourse around fossil fuel extraction has also been extensively studied, particularly as the fracking boom progressed and engendered controversy-though not always through the lens of social movement studies. Instead, scholars have often drawn on social representations theory-which postulates that social representations (in some sense frames) make the unfamiliar (e.g., LNG) familiar by drawing on existing worldviews and making it concrete using images or metaphors. Social representations are "common sense understandings of complex, often scientific, phenomena, generated in the public sphere and reliant on the history, culture, and social structure of the context in which they emerge" (Evensen et al., 2014, p. 66;Evensen & Stedman, 2016).

Often done using analysis of media coverage (as opposed to comments by social movement actors as part of siting processes), these scholars are not so much studying the framing process as they are the prevalence of frames. Diehl and McFarland (2010) distinguish between the processual focus of social movements' scholars-the meaning construction process of framing-and this structural focus of scholars in communication and social psychology on pre-given frames. This latter focus often examines the link between different frames and their effects on public opinion and preferences.

Like past environmental debates, media discourse around fracking has been dominated by a framing contest that pits economic gains against environmental impacts (Bigl, 2017;Matthews & Hansen, 2018;Mercado et al., 2014). As outlined in Matthews & Hansen, 2018, these competing discourses of "economic opportunity" on the one hand against "environmental threat" on the other came to dominate coverage across many different national contexts, as evidenced by research in the US and Europe (Bigl, 2017;Blair et al., 2015;Bomberg, 2017;Buttny & Feldpausch-Parker, 2016;Jaspal et al., 2014;Jaspal & Nerlich, 2014;Metze & Dodge, 2016). For example, in New York and Pennsylvania, Evensen et al. (2014) found media coverage to be dominated by themes related to environmental impacts (specifically water quality) and economic gains (jobs, leases, and royalties); social impacts were rarely covered. Similarly, Bomberg (2017) uncovered two broad storylines associated with fracking discourse in the UK: (1) an "opportunity" storyline offered by a pro-shale coalition of actors touting fracking's benefits in terms of economic growth, energy independence, and lower carbon emissions from shifting from coal to gas, as well as reassurances that fracking could be done in a safe manner and (2) a "threat" storyline offered by an antishale coalition of actors concerned about risks to human health, the environment, landscape, climate and democratic practices. She goes on to argue that the anti-fracking discourse was more successful, in part because opponents "…expanded the debate beyond economic or environmental concerns to include potent issues of local power and democracy" (p. 86). Given the prevalence of these two fracking storylines in multiple locations and the inherent association between fracking and export terminals, we might expect to see a similar dichotomy in the framing of LNG facilities in Oregon-though the particulars of what types of environmental impacts are raised will likely vary with the potential risks of the technology and extant local concerns. Moreover, as Bomberg's findings indicate, to the extent one side can enlarge the debate beyond economic or environmental issues, their frames may prove more resonant with members of the public.

While LNG siting discourse has received less attention in the scholarly literature, there are a few exceptions. Chen and Gunster (2016) analyzed pro-LNG information sources in British Columbia, Canada, and found two main themes: one emphasizing economic benefits and the other the "clean" nature of natural gas when compared to other fossil fuels, echoing some of the same frames found surrounding fracking. Examining media coverage specific to the Pacific LNG export project in British Columbia, Chen, 2020 again uncovered a framing contest pitting the economy versus the environment-this time stoked by pro-LNG forces and particularly prevalent in commercial media. Independent media outlets, instead, focused on environmental impacts of the proposed project, as well as fears about the industry's influence over government decision-makers and democratic accountability, particularly as related to the rights of Indigenous peoples. Tran et al.'s (2019) analysis of LNG proposals in Clatsop County, Oregon, highlighted how opponents moved beyond the traditional economy versus environment debate to raise concerns about the proposals' impacts on the area's burgeoning tourism and recreational industry. Moreover, they shifted their framing of the proposals' potential public safety impacts to align with changes in national and regional discourse, from relating to terrorism to earthquakes.

Our research seeks to complement the existing scholarship with a specific emphasis on the processual notion of framing, examining the dynamic nature of frames by utilizing recent advancements in computational text analysis to track framing over time. Moreover, by relying on the comments of active members in the public in both public hearings and through letters to the editor, as opposed to media coverage, we can more closely approximate the views of the active public (Dokshin, 2021). Media outlets tend to have a bias toward elite actors (e.g., politicians and businesses) at the expense of environmental groups and often reflect the political leanings of the editorial board (Blair et al., 2016;Matthews & Hansen, 2018). While the public is undoubtedly influenced by media coverage and editors make choices about what letters to print, such comments should provide a more direct reflection of the frames that are circulating among those most active in the debate.

In particular, we seek to describe the framing dialogue between opponents and supporters of LNG in Oregon, as well as how these frames change over time and across different contexts and venues. Given the many dimensions of proposed LNG development, opponents and supporters have a wide array of frames to potentially use in advocating their positions. Further, the long timeframe of LNG proposals in Oregon has allowed ample time for changes in the prevalence of framing to be identified, both over time and across different venues. Our research seeks to use a new method for computational analysis of text data to address the following three research questions:

RQ1: What are the most prevalent topics in LNG siting discourse, and how do they vary over time? RQ2: How does topic prevalence differ for opponents and supporters? RQ3: How does topic prevalence vary across venue of comment (hearing vs. letter)? While our emphasis is largely descriptive, we also include a more detailed analysis in our discussion as to how and why particular frames gained relevance over time and by venue, as well as what our findings may mean for future framing contests around energy development.

Third, frames are developed through contested processes, as all those involved in a movement or counter-movement exchange frames with all other actors and therefore adapt frames as a result of framing contests.

Framing involves a "strategic dialogue intended to shape a particular group into a coherent movement" (Ryan & Gamson, 2016, p. 13). When grounded in an understanding of power relations, strategic use of framing can build and sustain a movement. Ryan and Gamson elaborated four key points about frames: (1) facts take on meaning by how they are embedded in frames; (2) people carry around multiple frames for an issue or event in their heads; (3) successful reframing involves understanding an adversary's worldview; and (4) all frames contain some appeal to moral principle, either implicit or explicit. However, Ryan and Gamson also caution that framing alone is insufficient to build a movement and efforts must be made within the context of movement-building and focusing on the carriers of frames, such as using framing to determine how to gain access or to facilitate collaboration between groups with similar goals.

More recent research on framing has sought to examine a wide range of social movements-at both the national and international level-and expand our understanding of framing strategies. For example, the concept of frame resonance has been further explored, particularly by examining the mechanisms through which frames fail to resonate, such as misalignment between frame and audience or exhaustion with overused or taken-for-granted frames (Snow & Corrigall-Brown, 2005). Other research has focused on how framing is used in resource mobilization by identifying why certain frames are emphasized to audiences from which an organization is hoping to receive donations (Wooten, 2010). Scholarship in this vein has focused on framing as a dynamic process that is always evolving-a departure from previous studies that too often treated frames as static (Benford, 1997).

In fact, Benford (1997) has critiqued past approaches to studying the role of framing in social movements, specifically noting that, "we lack systematic empirical studies across cases, movements, and time" (p. 411). In this article, we use computational text analysis to do just this-study the framing of LNG in Oregon over time and across cases in a systematic manner.

Discourse around fracking and fossil fuel development. Discourse around fossil fuel extraction has also been extensively studied, particularly as the fracking boom progressed and engendered controversy-though not always through the lens of social movement studies. Instead, scholars have often drawn on social representations theory-which postulates that social representations (in some sense frames) make the unfamiliar (e.g., LNG) familiar by drawing on existing worldviews and making it concrete using images or metaphors. Social representations are "common sense understandings of complex, often scientific, phenomena, generated in the public sphere and reliant on the history, culture, and social structure of the context in which they emerge" (Evensen et al., 2014, p. 66;Evensen & Stedman, 2016).

Often done using analysis of media coverage (as opposed to comments by social movement actors as part of siting processes), these scholars are not so much studying the framing process as they are the prevalence of frames. Diehl and McFarland (2010) distinguish between the processual focus of social movements' scholars-the meaning construction process of framing-and this structural focus of scholars in communication and social psychology on pre-given frames. This latter focus often examines the link between different frames and their effects on public opinion and preferences.

Like past environmental debates, media discourse around fracking has been dominated by a framing contest that pits economic gains against environmental impacts (Bigl, 2017;Matthews & Hansen, 2018;Mercado et al., 2014). As outlined in Matthews & Hansen, 2018, these competing discourses of "economic opportunity" on the one hand against "environmental threat" on the other came to dominate coverage across many different national contexts, as evidenced by research in the US and Europe (Bigl, 2017;Blair et al., 2015;Bomberg, 2017;Buttny & Feldpausch-Parker, 2016;Jaspal et al., 2014;Jaspal & Nerlich, 2014;Metze & Dodge, 2016). For example, in New York and Pennsylvania, Evensen et al. (2014) found media coverage to be dominated by themes related to environmental impacts (specifically water quality) and economic gains (jobs, leases, and royalties); social impacts were rarely covered. Similarly, Bomberg (2017) uncovered two broad storylines associated with fracking discourse in the UK: (1) an "opportunity" storyline offered by a pro-shale coalition of actors touting fracking's benefits in terms of economic growth, energy independence, and lower carbon emissions from shifting from coal to gas, as well as reassurances that fracking could be done in a safe manner and (2) a "threat" storyline offered by an antishale coalition of actors concerned about risks to human health, the environment, landscape, climate and democratic practices. She goes on to argue that the anti-fracking discourse was more successful, in part because opponents "…expanded the debate beyond economic or environmental concerns to include potent issues of local power and democracy" (p. 86). Given the prevalence of these two fracking storylines in multiple locations and the inherent association between fracking and export terminals, we might expect to see a similar dichotomy in the framing of LNG facilities in Oregon-though the particulars of what types of environmental impacts are raised will likely vary with the potential risks of the technology and extant local concerns. Moreover, as Bomberg's findings indicate, to the extent one side can enlarge the debate beyond economic or environmental issues, their frames may prove more resonant with members of the public.

While LNG siting discourse has received less attention in the scholarly literature, there are a few exceptions. Chen and Gunster (2016) analyzed pro-LNG information sources in British Columbia, Canada, and found two main themes: one emphasizing economic benefits and the other the "clean" nature of natural gas when compared to other fossil fuels, echoing some of the same frames found surrounding fracking. Examining media coverage specific to the Pacific LNG export project in British Columbia, Chen, 2020 again uncovered a framing contest pitting the economy versus the environment-this time stoked by pro-LNG forces and particularly prevalent in commercial media. Independent media outlets, instead, focused on environmental impacts of the proposed project, as well as fears about the industry's influence over government decision-makers and democratic accountability, particularly as related to the rights of Indigenous peoples. Tran et al.'s (2019) analysis of LNG proposals in Clatsop County, Oregon, highlighted how opponents moved beyond the traditional economy versus environment debate to raise concerns about the proposals' impacts on the area's burgeoning tourism and recreational industry. Moreover, they shifted their framing of the proposals' potential public safety impacts to align with changes in national and regional discourse, from relating to terrorism to earthquakes.

Our research seeks to complement the existing scholarship with a specific emphasis on the processual notion of framing, examining the dynamic nature of frames by utilizing recent advancements in computational text analysis to track framing over time. Moreover, by relying on the comments of active members in the public in both public hearings and through letters to the editor, as opposed to media coverage, we can more closely approximate the views of the active public (Dokshin, 2021). Media outlets tend to have a bias toward elite actors (e.g., politicians and businesses) at the expense of environmental groups and often reflect the political leanings of the editorial board (Blair et al., 2016;Matthews & Hansen, 2018). While the public is undoubtedly influenced by media coverage and editors make choices about what letters to print, such comments should provide a more direct reflection of the frames that are circulating among those most active in the debate.

In particular, we seek to describe the framing dialogue between opponents and supporters of LNG in Oregon, as well as how these frames change over time and across different contexts and venues. Given the many dimensions of proposed LNG development, opponents and supporters have a wide array of frames to potentially use in advocating their positions. Further, the long timeframe of LNG proposals in Oregon has allowed ample time for changes in the prevalence of framing to be identified, both over time and across different venues. Our research seeks to use a new method for computational analysis of text data to address the following three research questions:

RQ1: What are the most prevalent topics in LNG siting discourse, and how do they vary over time? RQ2: How does topic prevalence differ for opponents and supporters? RQ3: How does topic prevalence vary across venue of comment (hearing vs. letter)? While our emphasis is largely descriptive, we also include a more detailed analysis in our discussion as to how and why particular frames gained relevance over time and by venue, as well as what our findings may mean for future framing contests around energy development.

paragraphs: Oregon Liquefied Natural Gas Siting. Since 2004, Oregon has experienced six LNG terminal proposals, though no project has yet to reach construction (Figure 1). The proposed facilities have focused on two primary locations: Astoria, a small city in northwest Oregon with a population around 10,000 people, and Coos Bay, a city of roughly 16,000 on the southern Oregon coast. Initially, companies sought to import LNG prior to the expansion of natural gas production in the U.S., with two projects in the greater Astoria area (Bradwood Landing in Astoria and Oregon LNG in neighboring Warrenton) and one in Coos Bay (Jordan Cove Energy Project). After initial hearings in 2007 and 2008, the Oregon LNG import facility failed to advance far enough in the permitting process for an official decision by federal regulators. Both Bradwood Landing and Jordan Cove received federal approval to be built, but the changing economics afforded by the U.S. fracking boom prompted corporate backers to withdraw these plans. In the case of Bradwood Landing, the project sponsor ultimately declared bankruptcy and abandoned LNG siting altogether. Project sponsors for Oregon LNG and Jordan Cove re-applied in 2011 for proposed export facilities, but this time neither proposal received approval: the Oregon LNG project was withdrawn; Jordan Cove was rejected by regulators. However, the Jordan Cove proposal in Coos Bay was revived in 2017, backed by a new corporate sponsor, and sought approval from the Trump Administration. Though the proposal received federal approval in March 2020, the Jordan Cove project has since been unable to obtain necessary state permits and is currently "on pause" according to the project sponsor (S&P Global, 2021).

The primary agency responsible for reviewing and approving onshore LNG terminal construction-and any corresponding pipeline infrastructure-is the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC). Under the Natural Gas Act of 1938 and the Energy Policy Act of 2005, FERC is responsible for the approval process of proposed LNG terminals, including the preparation of environmental impact statements (EISs) as required by the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). The review process incorporates expertise of a variety of agencies, as well as public comments through initial scoping hearings and follow-up hearings between the issuance of the draft EIS and preparation of the final EIS. While state and local permits are still required for construction to begin, FERC has the sole authority to approve projects at the federal level.

Throughout the FERC approval processes in the six LNG terminal proposals in Oregon, opponents and supporters of proposed development have sought to mobilize communities for their cause. Opponents founded several local organizations, such as Citizens Against LNG and RiverVision, and worked with other existing groups such as Columbia Riverkeeper and Rogue Climate, in order to fight proposed terminals. These groups organized demonstrations, fundraising events, and coordinated public hearing testimony (Tran et al., 2019). Additionally, both sides sought to broadcast their views about proposed terminals to the broader community via the opinion sections of local newspapers, submitting close to two thousand letters to the editor over the 16-year period.

Our objective is to examine the thousands of publicly available text comments generated during the six siting processes through the lens of environmental social movements, particularly focusing on the use of framing to mobilize resources and target political opportunities.

Topic Modeling Approach. To answer our research questions, we use computational text analysis of publicly available hearing transcripts and letter to the editor. Text content analysis has been a common research method for the study of framing in media and public comments (e.g., Farrell, 2016;Chandelier et al., 2018). The advantage of computational text analysis to traditional qualitative coding is the ability to process large quantities of data and identify statistical trends at scale in a shorter period of time (Müller-Hansen et al., 2020). While a variety of computational text analysis approaches may be useful for social research, our analysis specifically uses topic modeling to identify frames in LNG siting comments. Topic modeling is an unsupervised machine learning approach, meaning that topics are identified from the collection of documents without prior coding by the researcher. The approach utilizes a "bag of words" representation, which reduces each text document (e.g., hearing comment or letter to the editor) to a vector that counts the occurrence of all words found in the full collection of documents (Roberts et al., 2016). Topic modeling assumes that documents are composed of a limited number of latent topics, with topics in turn being comprised of words that frequently appear together in the same document. Our analysis uses a type of topic modeling called structural topic modeling (STM), which differentiates from other topic modeling approaches in its ability to utilize document-associated metadata (e.g., type of comment, location of comment, and stance of speaker) via generalized linear models in generating topics (Roberts et al., 2016). In other words, STM allows researchers to not only identify topics but to examine the context in which they appear at different frequencies.

Description of the Data. We obtained the text data used for analysis from two sources. First, we downloaded transcripts from the FERC eLibrary for all public hearings held by the regulatory agency for all six LNG terminal proposals in Oregon. Across the 60 20 100 0 100 0 15 10 75 2005 169 72 28 100 0 14 5 82 2006 236 32 68 32 68 15 8 77 2007 564 38 62 100 0 4 5 91 2008 637 45 55 65 35 10 5 85 2009 346 84 16 91 9 15 11 74 2010 127 100 0 83 17 10 11 79 2011 63 100 0 97 3 10 3 87 2012 408 18 82 49 51 25 5 69 2013 74 100 0 54 46 12 16 72 2014 605 35 65 11 89 27 6 67 2015 216 27 73 99 1 6 2 93 2016 69 100 0 39 61 9 16 75 2017 357 20 80 2 98 33 3 63 2018 93 100 0 9 91 24 3 73 Total 4618 42 58 49 51 18 5

hearings, we identified a total of 7625 unique comments using an automated procedure to scrape text after each identified speaker within the transcript. Second, we obtained all editorials and letters to the editor printed by local newspapers in LNG siting communities from November 2004, the initial media coverage of LNG import in Astoria, through July 2019, the month after the final FERC hearing. The letters were identified by keyword search in the NewsBank data repository for any article that contained either "LNG" or "liquefied natural gas" in the two primary local newspapers in proposed LNG sites-The Daily Astorian and The Coos Bay World-and we retained only those identified as opinion or editorial. In order to limit the analysis to the most relevant documents, we then applied the following criteria: (1) upon recommendation of Banks et al. (2018), we filtered out any documents comprised of fewer than 200 characters of text;

(2) we removed comments from official moderators and FERC presenters at the various hearings in order to focus only on public comments; and (3) we removed any remaining documents that lacked substantive text content, such as hearing comments discussing whether speakers on the sign-up sheet were currently present in the audience. Our final set of documents for analysis consisted of 4618 documents, including 2686 hearing comments and 1932 letters and editorials.

Table 1 shows the occurrence of text documents by year, type, location, and stance. The sporadic occurrence of FERC hearings results in an uneven distribution in the total number of text documents (Figure 2), as the occurrence of a hearing resulted in a high number of text comments, yet there were 6 years in which no hearings were held. Letters and editorials, on the other hand, occur in a more constant pattern, albeit with lower total comments. In terms of location, LNG terminal siting began in the greater Astoria region 2 years before Coos Bay. Additionally, the existence of two LNG import terminal proposals in the Astoria area compared to the single proposal in Coos Bay meant that the majority of comments through 2011 (the import phase of proposals) occurred in Astoria. But during the export phase (from 2012 onwards), most comments and letters occurred in Coos Bay because of two export proposals at Jordan Cove compared to the single Oregon LNG proposal at Astoria. Finally, the stance of comments and letters exhibited the least change over time, as opponents outnumbered supporters and neutrals by at least 2-to-1 in almost every year, though supporter comments and letters became more frequent during export proposals, particularly during the second siting attempt at Jordan Cove (2017)(2018)(2019).

paragraphs: To convert the comments and letters into a format for analysis, we applied a series of text pre-processing tools using the textProcessor package in R. Again following best practices (Banks et al., 2018), we removed common stop-words, retained only alphabet characters, and used stemming to reduce similar words to their common roots (e.g., "community" and "communities"). Through an iterative process, we identified additional custom stop-words (e.g., "thank" and "you," which commonly appeared in hearing transcripts) and removed from the documents in order to focus on the most relevant words. Additionally, we used a similar iterative approach to establish a lower threshold for sparse terms-terms that appeared in only a handful of the 4618 documents-ultimately using a minimum of 50 documents (or just over 1% of all documents). To conduct the analysis, we used the stm package in R. We examined model runs with topic numbers from 10 to 50, ultimately selecting the 23-topic model. Models were specified with spectral initialization and document-associated metadata consisted of document date, project location (either Astoria or Coos Bay), type of comment (either hearing or letter), and stance of speaker (support, oppose, or neutral). Comment stance was coded manually by authors for all 4618 comments and letters. We present the final topics generated, group them into analytical categories, and compare the prevalence of topics over time within the three major analytical categories of interest: threats, economics, and process. Finally, we use document metadataspecifically stance of speaker and venue of comment-to compare the likelihood of topics appearing in different contexts. We present figures showing the difference in topic prevalence, with corresponding 95% confidence intervals, for supporters versus opponents and hearing comments versus letters.

sections.paper_id: Stelmach and Boudet - 2022 - Using Structural Topic Modeling to Explore the Role of Framing in Shaping the Debate on Liquefied Na
Stelmach and Boudet - 2022 - Using Structural Topic Modeling to Explore the Role of Framing in Shaping the Debate on Liquefied Na
paragraphs: Our final model consisted of 23 topics, which are presented and described in Table 2. For each topic, we provide a label, a description of the topic, and the most commonly occurring words in the topic. In order to better understand the topics, we analytically grouped them into four broader categories of topics: (1) topics that address the economics of LNG terminal siting; (2) topics that address potential threats of LNG terminal FREX refers to the words that are both frequent and exclusive, meaning they are words that distinguish topics from one another (Airoldi & Bischof, 2016).
development;

(3) topics that address aspects of the LNG terminal siting process; and (4) topics that consist of either informal speech or descriptions of place. Figure 3 presents the relative proportion of topics by each composite grouping over time. Initially, topics related to threats accounted for 40% of all comments and letters, but within a few years dropped down to around 20% from 2008 to 2011 before steadily increasing back to around 40% by 2019. Topics related to process comprised the second-highest proportion in early LNG terminal siting, ultimately becoming the most common type of topic, coinciding with the decline in threat topics. Economic topics accounted for only about 10% of the content of comments and letters early on, but more than doubled in occurrence over time. Finally, descriptive topics increased in prevalence over time.

Threats. Next, we explore the composition of each category of topics, beginning with threats (Figure 4). Early on in LNG terminal siting, the two most prevalent threats discussed in hearings and letters were concerns over LNG tanker safety-either unintentional accidents or intentional terrorist attacks-and discussion of negative community impacts, such as impacts to public health and loss of local tourism. However, by 2006, threat discourse rapidly shifted to discussion of pipeline risksboth in terms of safety and how they would impact the land they crossed. Pipeline discourse then shifted to specific impacts of pipeline construction, emphasizing the threat to private property rights due to the use of eminent domain to navigate through private lands, as well as the damages to ecological habitats that could be caused by stream and river crossings.

The shift to LNG export facilities is associated with a rapid increase in discussion of climate change, as very few comments and letters referenced climate change until 2012. Yet by the end of our dataset, climate change was the most prevalent threat of the eight topics. Opponents urged FERC to consider downstream carbon emissions in their assessment of the environmental impacts, a calculation that FERC has only started to consider in subsequent natural gas infrastructure projects (FERC, 2021). Similarly, opponents appealed to FERC to prioritize greener energy infrastructure. One commenter at a 2017 hearing urged, "[w]e have to start making the transition, and we can't make that transition at a time when we're putting tons of money into fossil fuel infrastructure." Additionally, seismic concerns increased significantly starting in 2011the same year as the nuclear disaster at Fukushima that was caused by a tsunami. Concerns about earthquakes and tsunamis remained prevalent as media attention increased due to research around the probability of a high-magnitude earthquake in the Cascadia Subduction Zone (Schulz, 2015). As a result, seismic concerns overtook LNG tanker safety and pipeline safety as the main framing related to catastrophic events. Economics. The composition of economics topics over time is presented in Figure 5. Discussion of natural gas prices and markets accounted for almost half of all economics topics early in LNG siting processes. Supporters argued that the construction of LNG terminals would help secure a cheap energy supply, while opponents warned that connecting to an international market for natural gas would risk increasing prices in the long run. As LNG proposals shifted to export terminals, opponents again raised concerns over market connections raising domestic prices, while supporters argued that exporting LNG would benefit the global economy by connecting Asian countries with a cheaper (and cleaner) energy source than coal. Initial economics topics also focused on community revenue and jobs but quickly transitioned to a discussion of the need for LNG. During the import phase, opponents noted the construction of a similar import terminal in Baja California (Mexico), as well as government reports suggesting the market for LNG import facilities was already sufficient. The changing from import proposals to export led to a significant jump in discussion of jobs, particularly during FERC hearings on export, with high representation from construction unions. In later years without any hearings-particularly 2013, 2016, and 2018-community revenue replaced jobs as a more prevalent economic topic, as supporters consistently touted the project's proposed community enhancement plan that they claimed would deliver tens of millions of dollars to Southern Oregon county governments. As one supporter put it in a 2014 editorial in The Coos Bay World, "…with proper oversight, the windfall could build an endowment that will generate income for generations."

Process. Figure 6 shows the composition of process topics over time. Discussion of how the Port of Astoria acted in approving a lease for LNG terminal construction without public consultation dominated early process topics. In a 2004 letter just after the announcement, one opponent wrote:

"The Port of Astoria Commissioners decided that we, the people, would not know anything at all about its plans for the liquefied natural gas plant. I understand that the public meetings do not allow for any citizen input. This is unacceptable. We, the citizens, have a right to know what the rulers have in mind for us. Let's not allow our elected representatives to get away with such antics."

Given the lack of public consultation, the lease announcement came as a shock to the community and greatly compromised the trust between community members and local government, spurring initial mobilization and thus becoming an early focal point. Process topics quickly shifted to a focus on FERC-related topics, including pipeline routing, discussion of FERC's conduct in preparing an environmental impact statement (EIS) for a proposed terminal, and formal questioning of FERC's role and responsibility in siting decisions. Discussion of local politics quickly emerged as the most prevalent process topic, accounting for approximately half of all process discourse from 2009 through 2011. During this time period, local activists sought to focus efforts to create their own political opportunities, with the attempted recall of three county commissioners who approved the Port of Astoria leases for LNG development (Tran et al., 2019). While the recall efforts only successfully recalled one of the commissioners, the remaining two were ousted during the next election cycle, hence the extended focus on local politics. Following the success of the Astoria emphasis on local politics, Coos Bay also featured efforts by opponents (and supporters) to utilize city and county elections as battlegrounds of LNG development, though not to the level of organization and intensity as in Astoria. The FERC EIS process also emerged as a major point of emphasis during the export phase, as the public criticized the limited scope of FERC's environmental review, in particular questioning the lack of consideration of upstream and downstream greenhouse gas emissions in the EIS preparation.

Discussion of state and federal control over the siting process also increased over time, as opponents questioned the seeming power imbalance between the state of Oregon and a federal agency and implored the state to exercise its power to block necessary permits. At a hearing in Astoria in 2015, one opponent explicitly invoked the notion of control, stating to the FERC "[t]he proposal to me is fundamentally a question of our sovereignty. Of whether our nation founded in bloody revolution that overthrew the control of foreign owners shall now succumb to the interest of billionaires…" And in a letter to The Daily Astorian in 2016, another opponent wrote, "[e]ach of us needs to urge Governor Brown to tell the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) and state agencies to honor and enforce Clatsop County's denial of the permit for Oregon LNG's proposed pipeline. Without a pipeline, there can be no terminal." While the former represents an expression of outrage directly aimed at the federal regulator, the latter represents a call to action to the broader community.

Topic Prevalence Comparisons. Next, we sought to examine the occurrence of identified topics across two primary comparisons of document metadata: stance and venue. Figure 7a presents the occurrence of economics topics for opponents and supporters. Supporters were much more likely to discuss jobs, while also significantly more likely (to a lesser extent) to mention community revenue and natural gas prices. Opponents were significantly more likely to discuss LNG need, particularly in the import phase when opponents questioned why other LNG infrastructure on the Pacific Coast already under construction was not sufficient for energy needs. At a hearing in Woodburn in 2008, one opponent questioned, "so if there is an over capacity now in the United States, and there is one being built in Mexico that will give the same capacity as all three of them proposed in Oregon, why are we even looking at this?" Figure 7b similarly presents the comparison of threats. Opponents were significantly more likely to discuss six of the eight threats, with the largest margins for climate change, private property rights, and seismic concerns. Supporters mentioned negative community impacts slightly more than opponents-though not to a statistically significant degree. While opponent comments about negative community impacts questioned the effects on public health and the threat LNG development posed to existing tourism industries, supporters tended to argue that tourism was not sufficient for local economic development. This type of supporter framing is best exemplified in an editorial by The Coos Bay World's editorial team in 2012:

"Ask: Where on the Oregon Coast does a tourist-based economy support a population center of 25,000 people? Answer: Nowhere. … To lift our larger community from its persistent poverty will require a balanced foundation of service businesses, maritime commerce and traditional core industries."

In terms of process, opponents were more likely to discuss five of the seven topics relative to supporters, with the other two having no significant difference for either group (Figure 7c). Though targeting local politics was an established strategy of opponents, supporters referenced this topic just as frequently. Supporters in Coos Bay expressed endorsements of local leaders on record as in favor of Jordan Cove, while also criticizing state politicians such as U.S. Senator Jeff Merkley and Governor Kate Brown for lack of support.

Shifting to comparison by venue, all four economics frames varied significantly based on venue (Figure 8a). Discussion of jobs occurred more commonly during FERC hearings, while community revenue, natural gas prices, and LNG need were favored in letters and editorials. During hearings-and particularly those held for Jordan Cove export facilities-labor organizations made sure to have representatives make official comments to FERC affirming union support for the projects because of the jobs they would bring to the region. But in letters, supporters more commonly emphasized the tax revenues of LNG development and what it would allow county governments, through either extension of social services or attracting other industries. While some opponents brought up questions about how LNG development would affect local prices for natural gas, as well as concerns over being a pass-through for California's energy needs, these topics emerged more often in letters, such as an opponent letter to The Daily Astorian in 2009 that asked, "California, the state that is to benefit from this LNG, has already rejected five of these proposed LNG regasification plants. Why should Oregon do California's dirty work?" Figure 8b presents the effect of venue on threat topic occurrence. Discussion of pipeline impacts and safety was far more common in hearings than letters. Given FERC's role in approving the construction of pipeline, it makes sense that opponents would raise these concerns to FERC directly. Discussion of climate change also occurred more often in hearings than letters, particularly as opponents demanded FERC to consider the downstream carbon dioxide emissions of natural gas export in the preparation of environmental impact statements. Meanwhile, discussion of LNG tanker safety, seismic activity, and negative community impacts occurred more commonly in letters. In a 2017 letter to the Coos Bay World, one opponent emphasized the dangers of a worst-case scenario:

"Nevertheless, between 10,000 and 15,000 people will live within 2.2 miles [the suggested distance from the public per the Society of International Gas Tanker and Terminal Operators] from the proposed Jordan Cove terminal, and from the shipping channel used by its LNG carriers. Some people will even be inside the 1/3 mile instant-live-cremation zone. And the 9.2 earthquake likely to strike during the life of the terminal will destroy roads, bridges and utilities, so forget about the fire department."

Finally, Figure 8c presents the occurrence of process topics by venue. Discussion of FERC processes and pipeline routing occurred much more frequently at FERC hearings than in letters, as opponents sought to focus on issues most relevant to FERC. In letters, local politics were much more likely to be discussed, as opponents brought the fight to alternative channels outside the federal approval process. Discussion of state and federal control over LNG siting also occurred more frequently in letters, as opponents attempted to appeal to other political opportunity structures, such as state agencies in reviewing Clean Water Act and Coastal Zone Management Act compliance permits.

As LNG proposals shifted to export terminals, opponents again raised concerns over market connections raising domestic prices, while supporters argued that exporting LNG would benefit the global economy by connecting Asian countries with a cheaper (and cleaner) energy source than coal. Initial economics topics also focused on community revenue and jobs but quickly transitioned to a discussion of the need for LNG. During the import phase, opponents noted the construction of a similar import terminal in Baja California (Mexico), as well as government reports suggesting the market for LNG import facilities was already sufficient. The changing from import proposals to export led to a significant jump in discussion of jobs, particularly during FERC hearings on export, with high representation from construction unions. In later years without any hearings-particularly 2013, 2016, and 2018-community revenue replaced jobs as a more prevalent economic topic, as supporters consistently touted the project's proposed community enhancement plan that they claimed would deliver tens of millions of dollars to Southern Oregon county governments. As one supporter put it in a 2014 editorial in The Coos Bay World, "…with proper oversight, the windfall could build an endowment that will generate income for generations."

Process. Figure 6 shows the composition of process topics over time. Discussion of how the Port of Astoria acted in approving a lease for LNG terminal construction without public consultation dominated early process topics. In a 2004 letter just after the announcement, one opponent wrote:

"The Port of Astoria Commissioners decided that we, the people, would not know anything at all about its plans for the liquefied natural gas plant. I understand that the public meetings do not allow for any citizen input. This is unacceptable. We, the citizens, have a right to know what the rulers have in mind for us. Let's not allow our elected representatives to get away with such antics."

Given the lack of public consultation, the lease announcement came as a shock to the community and greatly compromised the trust between community members and local government, spurring initial mobilization and thus becoming an early focal point. Process topics quickly shifted to a focus on FERC-related topics, including pipeline routing, discussion of FERC's conduct in preparing an environmental impact statement (EIS) for a proposed terminal, and formal questioning of FERC's role and responsibility in siting decisions. Discussion of local politics quickly emerged as the most prevalent process topic, accounting for approximately half of all process discourse from 2009 through 2011. During this time period, local activists sought to focus efforts to create their own political opportunities, with the attempted recall of three county commissioners who approved the Port of Astoria leases for LNG development (Tran et al., 2019). While the recall efforts only successfully recalled one of the commissioners, the remaining two were ousted during the next election cycle, hence the extended focus on local politics. Following the success of the Astoria emphasis on local politics, Coos Bay also featured efforts by opponents (and supporters) to utilize city and county elections as battlegrounds of LNG development, though not to the level of organization and intensity as in Astoria. The FERC EIS process also emerged as a major point of emphasis during the export phase, as the public criticized the limited scope of FERC's environmental review, in particular questioning the lack of consideration of upstream and downstream greenhouse gas emissions in the EIS preparation.

Discussion of state and federal control over the siting process also increased over time, as opponents questioned the seeming power imbalance between the state of Oregon and a federal agency and implored the state to exercise its power to block necessary permits. At a hearing in Astoria in 2015, one opponent explicitly invoked the notion of control, stating to the FERC "[t]he proposal to me is fundamentally a question of our sovereignty. Of whether our nation founded in bloody revolution that overthrew the control of foreign owners shall now succumb to the interest of billionaires…" And in a letter to The Daily Astorian in 2016, another opponent wrote, "[e]ach of us needs to urge Governor Brown to tell the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) and state agencies to honor and enforce Clatsop County's denial of the permit for Oregon LNG's proposed pipeline. Without a pipeline, there can be no terminal." While the former represents an expression of outrage directly aimed at the federal regulator, the latter represents a call to action to the broader community.

Topic Prevalence Comparisons. Next, we sought to examine the occurrence of identified topics across two primary comparisons of document metadata: stance and venue. Figure 7a presents the occurrence of economics topics for opponents and supporters. Supporters were much more likely to discuss jobs, while also significantly more likely (to a lesser extent) to mention community revenue and natural gas prices. Opponents were significantly more likely to discuss LNG need, particularly in the import phase when opponents questioned why other LNG infrastructure on the Pacific Coast already under construction was not sufficient for energy needs. At a hearing in Woodburn in 2008, one opponent questioned, "so if there is an over capacity now in the United States, and there is one being built in Mexico that will give the same capacity as all three of them proposed in Oregon, why are we even looking at this?" Figure 7b similarly presents the comparison of threats. Opponents were significantly more likely to discuss six of the eight threats, with the largest margins for climate change, private property rights, and seismic concerns. Supporters mentioned negative community impacts slightly more than opponents-though not to a statistically significant degree. While opponent comments about negative community impacts questioned the effects on public health and the threat LNG development posed to existing tourism industries, supporters tended to argue that tourism was not sufficient for local economic development. This type of supporter framing is best exemplified in an editorial by The Coos Bay World's editorial team in 2012:

"Ask: Where on the Oregon Coast does a tourist-based economy support a population center of 25,000 people? Answer: Nowhere. … To lift our larger community from its persistent poverty will require a balanced foundation of service businesses, maritime commerce and traditional core industries."

In terms of process, opponents were more likely to discuss five of the seven topics relative to supporters, with the other two having no significant difference for either group (Figure 7c). Though targeting local politics was an established strategy of opponents, supporters referenced this topic just as frequently. Supporters in Coos Bay expressed endorsements of local leaders on record as in favor of Jordan Cove, while also criticizing state politicians such as U.S. Senator Jeff Merkley and Governor Kate Brown for lack of support.

Shifting to comparison by venue, all four economics frames varied significantly based on venue (Figure 8a). Discussion of jobs occurred more commonly during FERC hearings, while community revenue, natural gas prices, and LNG need were favored in letters and editorials. During hearings-and particularly those held for Jordan Cove export facilities-labor organizations made sure to have representatives make official comments to FERC affirming union support for the projects because of the jobs they would bring to the region. But in letters, supporters more commonly emphasized the tax revenues of LNG development and what it would allow county governments, through either extension of social services or attracting other industries. While some opponents brought up questions about how LNG development would affect local prices for natural gas, as well as concerns over being a pass-through for California's energy needs, these topics emerged more often in letters, such as an opponent letter to The Daily Astorian in 2009 that asked, "California, the state that is to benefit from this LNG, has already rejected five of these proposed LNG regasification plants. Why should Oregon do California's dirty work?" Figure 8b presents the effect of venue on threat topic occurrence. Discussion of pipeline impacts and safety was far more common in hearings than letters. Given FERC's role in approving the construction of pipeline, it makes sense that opponents would raise these concerns to FERC directly. Discussion of climate change also occurred more often in hearings than letters, particularly as opponents demanded FERC to consider the downstream carbon dioxide emissions of natural gas export in the preparation of environmental impact statements. Meanwhile, discussion of LNG tanker safety, seismic activity, and negative community impacts occurred more commonly in letters. In a 2017 letter to the Coos Bay World, one opponent emphasized the dangers of a worst-case scenario:

"Nevertheless, between 10,000 and 15,000 people will live within 2.2 miles [the suggested distance from the public per the Society of International Gas Tanker and Terminal Operators] from the proposed Jordan Cove terminal, and from the shipping channel used by its LNG carriers. Some people will even be inside the 1/3 mile instant-live-cremation zone. And the 9.2 earthquake likely to strike during the life of the terminal will destroy roads, bridges and utilities, so forget about the fire department."

Finally, Figure 8c presents the occurrence of process topics by venue. Discussion of FERC processes and pipeline routing occurred much more frequently at FERC hearings than in letters, as opponents sought to focus on issues most relevant to FERC. In letters, local politics were much more likely to be discussed, as opponents brought the fight to alternative channels outside the federal approval process. Discussion of state and federal control over LNG siting also occurred more frequently in letters, as opponents attempted to appeal to other political opportunity structures, such as state agencies in reviewing Clean Water Act and Coastal Zone Management Act compliance permits.

sections.paper_id: Stelmach and Boudet - 2022 - Using Structural Topic Modeling to Explore the Role of Framing in Shaping the Debate on Liquefied Na
paragraphs: Our analysis reveals clear trends in the occurrence of topics over time, both by speaker stance on LNG and by venue of comment. At a general level, our text analysis identified a similar pattern of economics versus environment framing that has been found around fracking and other fossil fuel development (Chen & Gunster, 2016;Matthews & Hansen, 2018), with opponents emphasizing threats and supporters focusing on economic benefits. That being said, we identified a wider range of threats-both environmental (e.g., ecological impacts) and social (e.g., property rights)-compared to previous studies of media discourse around fracking, which has revealed a lack of coverage of social impacts from energy development in media discourse around fracking (Evensen et al., 2014). Our work exploring comments in hearing and letters in Oregon around LNG suggests that local activists may be more willing to raise potential social impacts, perhaps more so than traditional media (though this would require a comparison to traditional media coverage of LNG in Oregon) or because of the topic-LNG as opposed to fracking. Moreover, the disparity between opponents and supporters was not consistent across all four economics topics or all eight threats. Supporters contested some of the threat frames, particularly the discussion of negative community impacts. Opponents, meanwhile, questioned the need to construct an LNG terminal in Oregon given the nature of local gas markets and why such terminals were not proposed closer to markets in need, particularly when proposals were focused on import.

This changing composition of narratives around economics, threats, and process reveals a level of adaptation by opponent and supporter groups, as frames used by both groups fluctuated over time and by venue. Opponent use of threat frames initially focused on fears about low probability, high impact events to the local community, such as LNG tanker explosions and pipeline failures. Opponents soon increased emphasis on higher probability threats, such as ecological concerns and impacts of pipeline construction on private lands. Schaffer Boudet and Ortolano (2010), in studying LNG import facilities in California, found a similar, purposive shifting away from framing opposition as related to low probability, high consequence events, such as a terrorist attack, to higher probability impacts, such as adverse public health effects. In the export phase of proposals in Oregon, opponents continued this trend and began to emphasize the effects of LNG terminal construction on a topic of growing salience-climate change-which ultimately became the most prevalent threat at the final round of FERC hearings. The move to tie LNG siting to climate change allowed opponents to expand their framing to more widespread, global impacts and connect their efforts to the growing anti-fossil fuel movement.

Opponents were also able to bridge their framing efforts by pairing environmental framing with broader questions regarding local governance and how democratic the siting process was, allowing them to build a broader coalition of opposition. Schaffer Boudet (2011) linked the concept of frame bridging to that of brokerage-the strategic effort of movement actors to connect with other actors in an expanding movement in an attempt to shift the scale of conflict-in her study of mobilization against LNG import terminals in the early 2000s. In that analysis, frame bridging and brokerage were found in the same linked process of coalition building, bringing together disparate strands of opposition into a stronger, more effective movement. We observe a strong element of a similar frame bridging in Oregon across both LNG siting locations. In Astoria, outrage at the initial lease approval process sparked a wide range of opposition among the community questioning the lack of public input and transparency in awarding the lease to construct an LNG terminal. In Coos Bay, the controversy over the use of eminent domain to route the pipeline prompted conservative ranchers to work with liberal environmentalists out of shared desire to block the proposed LNG projects (Wheeling, 2018). In both cases, opponents successfully engaged in a wider variety of framing than the false dichotomy of environment versus economy. Further, we found that opponents overall were more flexible in their framing than supporters over the course of the six LNG siting proposals; opponents used a wider distribution of topics than supporters, as confirmed by calculating Shannon's entropy for both sets of comments and letters. Bomberg (2017) argued that opponents of fracking have been more successful in the UK because of their ability to unite a variety of frames into a comprehensive storyline, unlike fracking proponents. Given the success opponents have found in stymying all LNG proposals to date in Oregon, it appears that a similar connection may be at play.

At the same time as the move toward higher probability consequences, low probability, high consequence threats remained a focus for opponents-but were adapted to changing times and concerns. The broader national and regional context of perceived threats-initially terrorism in the early years due to the events of September 11 th , 2001, and then earthquakes and tsunamis due to increased awareness of the looming threat of the Cascadia subduction zone (brought to attention by the Fukushima disaster in 2011)-appears to have influenced which threats opponents emphasized in their framing. Snow and Corrigall-Brown (2005) described the notion of frame relevance as a crucial mechanism through which a frame resonates with its target audience and further argued that this mechanism is affected by political and cultural context that determines what is relevant in the moment. We find evidence of concerns about frame relevance in our analysis, with opponents successfully altering their framing both around environmental concerns and safety threats to better align with emerging concerns in the political and cultural context over the decade and a half of LNG siting proposals.

Our analysis also revealed a clear effort by opponents and supporters to tailor their use of frames to specific audiences based on venue. Supporters relied on two main types of project benefits frames-jobs and community revenue-and swapped between the two depending on venue. During FERC hearings, supporters emphasized the jobs that would be created by approval. As part of the EIS preparation, FERC considers socioeconomic impacts, with the agency's internal guidance manual for environmental report preparation particularly emphasizing employment impacts (FERC Office of Energy Projects, 2017). But in letters to the broader community, supporters emphasized the benefits to the overall community, primarily in the form of increased tax revenues. Job creation only directly benefits a select few people, whereas community revenues accrue for the entire city or county. Similarly, opponents employed a different set of both threat and process frames in hearings compared to letters.

When addressing FERC, opponents put greater emphasis on matters directly under FERC control, such as the impacts of pipeline construction and operation, as well as questions about FERC processes and their selection of pipeline routing. In letters, opponents engaged in more affective imagery, emphasizing potential catastrophes that could befall a potential LNG terminal. Such catastrophic frames can attract other community members into joining the opposition as a motivational frame. The focus on FERC-relevant topics seems to indicate that opponents used the public hearings to contribute their local knowledge and concerns to regulatory agencies. Yet over time, frustrations with a perceived lack of consideration for their concerns led opponents to question the legitimacy of the federal-led process. Opponents also used the wider audience available in letters to target political opportunities outside of formal hearings, particularly in discussion of local politics. The recall of the locally elected decisionmakers who approved the initial land lease for LNG construction in Astoria became a focal point for opponents, ultimately resulting in the recall of one county commissioner and the eventual defeat of the remaining commissioners in the subsequent round of elections (Tran et al., 2019).

sections.paper_id: Stelmach and Boudet - 2022 - Using Structural Topic Modeling to Explore the Role of Framing in Shaping the Debate on Liquefied Na
paragraphs: Almost two decades after the first proposals to site LNG terminals in Oregon, opponents of the projects have been far more successful than project supporters. None of the proposed LNG facilities have reached the point of construction, and public opinion polls have shown a majority of Oregonians opposed the most recent Jordan Cove proposal (Sickinger, 2018). Based upon our analysis, we believe this to be attributable to two main reasons. First, opponents successfully broadened the discourse beyond the environment versus economy paradigm by incorporating frames around local autonomy and the siting process, particularly the fairness of initial leasing decisions and the use of eminent domain to site corresponding pipelines. Focusing on additional frames allowed opponents' arguments to resonate with a broader spectrum of the public, including rural, conservative landowners. Second, the growing public concern over climate change and the use of fossil fuels over the course of LNG proposals allowed opponents to emphasize additional environmental threats beyond local ecology. A climate change framing allowed opponents to speak to environmental concerns at a larger scale, particularly as climate change became a major focus of national political discourse in the U.S. and the object of a growing anti-fossil fuel movement. Data from the Climate Change in the American Mind biannual surveys have found that the segment of the U.S. population that reports the highest level of concern over climate change almost tripled from 2014 to 2019 (Goldberg et al., 2020). Over the same timeframe, activists have organized in similar movements against fossil fuel developments, such as the leave-it-in-the-ground and thin green line (i.e., between fossil fuel extraction in the Mountain West and energy hungry Asian markets) campaigns, which have been particularly active in the Pacific Northwest (Boudet & Hazboun, 2019). By linking LNG development to climate change, opponents appear to have been able to resonate strongly with a population that is increasingly concerned-and mobilized-around climate change. However, it remains to be examined whether it was the local activists who moved to climate change, or if the shift was led by regional movement organizers. Connecting the results of computational text analysis to other analytic approaches-such as social network analysis-could provide an opportunity to examine the spread of framing approaches through activist networks.

In practice, our findings, combined with those from the study of fracking discourses, suggest that opponents of fossil fuel development should continue to expand their framing of the issues beyond environmental threats to include concerns about local autonomy and governance, which can have a broader appeal than environmental issues alone. At the same time, given growing concern about the climate crisis, framing fossil fuel development as antithetical to addressing carbon emissions appears to be growing in resonance, at least in the context of a largely progressive and environmentally leaning state like Oregon.

Our analysis of issue framing during LNG siting in Oregon also has important implications for the study of issue framing in social movements. The use of computational text analysis offers the opportunity to scale content analysis of public comments to cover thousands of documents, thus allowing meaningful comparisons using comment metadata. Our analysis focused only on time, venue, and stance of texts, showing clear differences in opponent and supporter framing of LNG issues over time and by venue. Computational text analysis involves a range of useful tools capable of analyzing the vast volume of comments and texts generated from various social movements and, through its computational approach, facilitates a systematic comparison of cases within a broader movement or comparison of highly similar (or highly dissimilar) movements.

Given that our analysis examined two different site locations, we would expect to find that spatial context is an important driver of framing-perhaps even at smaller scales, such as different frames used by opponents closer to the proposed terminal than frames used by those closer to the proposed pipeline route. Future analysis should focus on the extent to which frame occurrence coincides with project permit decisions or protest events. Snow and Benford (1988) theorized that successful mobilization depends upon frames achieving the three core tasks of diagnosis, prognosis, and motivation, elaborating that "the more the three tasks are robust or richly developed and interconnected, the more successful the mobilization effort, ceterus peribus" (p. 199). Some process-oriented topics, such as local politics and state/ federal control, appeared to be oriented toward both prognosis and motivation, as they both identified strategies and tactics for successful opposition, such as urging state politicians to direct agencies to deny permits, but also included an element of a call-toarms. First, we utilized an unstructured approach for our computational text analysis, allowing the algorithm to identify topics with only minimal a priori specifications. Perhaps a more structured approach that incorporates either sentiment analysis or a researcherdeveloped dictionary of diagnostic, prognostic, and motivational words could identify which topics better fit each framing task. Another approach would be to examine the composition of words within each topic and see if certain words are more prevalent in different venues, such as increased diagnostic framing in hearings and increased motivational framing in letters. One final possibility is that Snow and Benford's hypothesis does not apply in the context of energy facility siting. Grimmer and Stewart (2013) cautioned against viewing automated text analysis as a panacea that replaces human qualitative analysis, noting that the complex and hidden process by which text is created means that no algorithm is capable of providing completely accurate accounts of the text generation process. Following Grimmer and Stewart's guidance, we sought to validate the findings from our understanding of the six LNG siting proposals to ensure the final analysis presented was a genuine representation of opponent and supporter comments. In addition to specifications within the modeling approach, the selection of which text documents to include can influence the results of topic modeling. Our aim was to capture two primary streams of communication; however, these two venues are far from the complete population of comments related to the proposals. In addition to the federal approval process presided over by FERC, LNG terminal siting includes several state and local permits that incorporate public comments. Analyzing the full set of state-level comments is difficult, however, as public availability and formatting of these comments varies across agency and LNG project. Almost two decades after the first proposals to site LNG terminals in Oregon, opponents of the projects have been far more successful than project supporters. None of the proposed LNG facilities have reached the point of construction, and public opinion polls have shown a majority of Oregonians opposed the most recent Jordan Cove proposal (Sickinger, 2018). Based upon our analysis, we believe this to be attributable to two main reasons. First, opponents successfully broadened the discourse beyond the environment versus economy paradigm by incorporating frames around local autonomy and the siting process, particularly the fairness of initial leasing decisions and the use of eminent domain to site corresponding pipelines. Focusing on additional frames allowed opponents' arguments to resonate with a broader spectrum of the public, including rural, conservative landowners. Second, the growing public concern over climate change and the use of fossil fuels over the course of LNG proposals allowed opponents to emphasize additional environmental threats beyond local ecology. A climate change framing allowed opponents to speak to environmental concerns at a larger scale, particularly as climate change became a major focus of national political discourse in the U.S. and the object of a growing anti-fossil fuel movement. Data from the Climate Change in the American Mind biannual surveys have found that the segment of the U.S. population that reports the highest level of concern over climate change almost tripled from 2014 to 2019 (Goldberg et al., 2020). Over the same timeframe, activists have organized in similar movements against fossil fuel developments, such as the leave-it-in-the-ground and thin green line (i.e., between fossil fuel extraction in the Mountain West and energy hungry Asian markets) campaigns, which have been particularly active in the Pacific Northwest (Boudet & Hazboun, 2019). By linking LNG development to climate change, opponents appear to have been able to resonate strongly with a population that is increasingly concerned-and mobilized-around climate change.
